schools.summary: This Aphek (distinct from the towns of the same name) lies in the plain of Jezreel near Shunem; the fountain is Ain Jalud, and the LXX's 'En-dor' is corrected.
Gill weighs which Aphek is meant and settles on one near Jezreel and Shunem.
Matthew Henry: David is in a 'great strait' of his own making, yet God will not let him be tempted above what he can bear.

themes: Aphek and the fountain of Jezreel

schools.summary: The orderly Philistine advance by hundreds and thousands; David marches in the rear-guard with Achish, a vassal owing military service for Ziklag, and his Hebrew arms draw notice.
Gill clarifies that the 'lords' each march at the head of their own hundreds and thousands.
The objectors are the confederate princes, not Achish's courtiers; 'these days, or these years' is an indefinite span the versions render variously.
Gill: the objectors are the other lords of the pentapolis, free to override Achish.
Wesley expands the phrase — 'did I say days? I might have said years' — reckoning David's time in Philistia and a possible earlier correspondence with Achish.

debates.question: How long had David been with Achish — 'days, or years'?

sides.position: an indefinite 'considerable time,' the versions varying
literally years — a year and four months now, plus an earlier sojourn

schools.summary: The princes fear David will turn 'satan' in the battle and buy Saul's favour with Philistine heads; 'go down' is the technical word for descending to the plain; they had not yet heard of David's Ziklag raids.
Gill: the princes' wrath fell on Achish for defending David.
Wesley: the princes reason by sound policy, yet God's gracious providence works through their suspicion to rescue David and speed him home.
The women's song of 1 Samuel 18:7 grounds the suspicion; Ellicott notes the Philistines, a musical people, preserved Israel's war-songs, and David learned Philistine music.
Gill: the song was sung 'long ago,' yet still shapes David's present.
'As the LORD liveth' is a striking oath on a Philistine's lips; Ellicott and Keil read it as courtesy toward David or the king's equivalent for his own gods.
Matthew Henry: Achish, one of five and over-voted, gives David a generous testimonial Saul never would; God's people should earn the good word of all.
Clarke: the praise rests on ignorance — Achish had not heard that David deceived him about fighting Israel.
Gill: Achish, being near, calls David to himself.

debates.question: What does Achish's oath 'as the LORD liveth' reveal?

sides.position: a real leaning toward the God of Israel, learned from David
diplomatic courtesy — swearing by David's God to reassure him

schools.summary: 'Go in peace' — Achish, bound by the princes' will, sends David back to Ziklag before the battle.
Gill: David is dismissed to Ziklag, released from the march.
Matthew Henry: this is the door opened for David's deliverance from a strait in which either course was sin.
David's protest 'rings of falseness,' but 'my lord the king' is ambiguous (Achish or Saul), so his words deceive without strictly lying; Ellicott floats that David engineered his own dismissal.
Gill: David feigns concern though 'heartily glad' to be dismissed.
Wesley: 'deep dissimulation and flattery, no way to be justified'; Clarke: God's providence spared David a choice of two sins.

debates.question: Did David deliberately provoke his own dismissal?

sides.position: possibly — he engineered the princes' suspicion to escape an impossible position
no — he was simply caught and relieved, covering his gladness with courtly words

schools.summary: Achish's 'as an angel of God' is his strongest assurance of David's innocence before he yields to the princes' veto.
Clarke: the angel-language and the oath by Jehovah suggest Achish had learned truth from David, even favouring the Jewish faith; Wesley: he borrowed the title of angels from his Israelite neighbours.
Gill: for all the king's affection, the princes' veto stands — David shall not go up.

debates.question: Does Achish's 'angel of God' reveal devotion or courtesy?

sides.position: a heart half-taught the truth by David's company
borrowed Hebrew idiom used as graceful, acceptable courtesy

schools.summary: 'Thy master's servants' perplexes: David's men as Saul's subjects, or the recently-defected Manassites (1 Chronicles 12:19) whose arrival first roused suspicion; Clarke reads 'master' as Saul, gently telling David he is no vassal.
Gill: calling them Saul's servants carries the very objection of the lords — they might seize a chance to rejoin their king.

debates.question: Who are 'thy master's servants,' and who is the master?

sides.position: David's band as Saul's subjects, hence distrusted
the newly-come Manassites who first drew Philistine suspicion
Saul is the master; Achish delicately frees David from vassalage

schools.summary: The columns part — David back to Ziklag, the Philistines up to Jezreel; Keil (with Seb. Schmidt) hears David light of heart, having sinned neither against Achish nor Israel; Ellicott notes the unseen peril awaiting at Ziklag.
Gill: David and his men were as eager to leave as the Philistines were to send them.
